patient_summary.name: John Smith
patient_summary.clinical_summary: John Smith has a longstanding history of Crohn's disease or indeterminate colitis, historically controlled with oral mesalamine and showing repeated endoscopic remission. The record documents intermittent mild anemia and vitamin D deficiency, a history of uveitis, and a flare after loss of access to branded Lialda. During the latest period, prednisone was used as a bridge while prior authorizations were pursued for branded Lialda and guselkumab (Tremfya).

timeline.title: Inflammatory and nutritional monitoring
timeline.interpretation: Mild anemia with low-normal vitamin D; inflammatory markers not markedly elevated.

timeline.title: Colonoscopy and upper endoscopy
timeline.details: Terminal ileum and colon were normal without active inflammatory bowel disease. A small hiatal hernia and possible mild reflux disease were noted.

timeline.title: Inflammatory and nutritional monitoring
timeline.interpretation: Vitamin D deficiency and borderline-low hemoglobin; inflammatory markers remained low.

timeline.title: Colonoscopy
timeline.details: Normal colon with no visible active inflammation.

timeline.title: Inflammatory marker monitoring
timeline.interpretation: No biochemical evidence of significant systemic inflammation.

timeline.title: Hepatitis A and Tdap vaccination
timeline.details: Hepatitis A and tetanus/diphtheria/pertussis vaccines documented.

timeline.title: Colonoscopy with biopsies
timeline.details: Deep endoscopic remission. Pathology showed rare cryptitis in the right colon and rare Paneth cell metaplasia in the left colon, without dysplasia or granulomas.

timeline.title: IBD and nutritional monitoring
timeline.interpretation: Low inflammatory activity with persistent borderline anemia; vitamin D and B12 were adequate.

timeline.title: Lichen planus
timeline.details: Lichen planus was documented in the problem list.

timeline.title: IBD monitoring
timeline.interpretation: Inflammatory markers remained low; mild anemia persisted.

timeline.title: Possible increase in inflammatory activity
timeline.interpretation: Inflammatory markers were higher than prior values but still only modestly elevated.

timeline.title: Tremfya treatment planning
timeline.details: Guselkumab (Tremfya) was considered for escalation of Crohn's disease treatment. Prior authorization was submitted.

timeline.title: Crohn's disease flare after interruption of branded Lialda
timeline.details: The patient reported that generic mesalamine was ineffective and that branded Lialda had been stopped because of insurance coverage. Symptoms worsened after the interruption. The plan was to resubmit authorization for branded Lialda, use prednisone as a bridge, complete laboratory and stool testing, and consider Tremfya.

details.medication_access_issue: Mesalamine had been stopped approximately two months earlier because of insurance.

timeline.title: Tremfya authorization approved
timeline.details: Authorization for guselkumab was approved for the period 2052-06-10 through 2053-01-13.

timeline.title: Branded Lialda authorization approved
timeline.details: After an urgent resubmission documenting treatment failure with generic mesalamine and a clinical flare, branded Lialda was approved through 2053-06-18.

narrative_markdown:
# Longitudinal Clinical Summary — John Smith

John Smith is a male patient with longstanding Crohn's disease or indeterminate colitis and a history of uveitis. His inflammatory bowel disease was treated over time with sulfasalazine, Pentasa, oral mesalamine, and branded Lialda, with intermittent use of budesonide or prednisone. For several years, laboratory markers were low and serial colonoscopies showed sustained endoscopic remission.

## Disease course

A colonoscopy and upper endoscopy on 2042-11-05 showed no active inflammatory bowel disease, with a normal terminal ileum and colon. A small hiatal hernia and possible mild reflux were noted. Colonoscopy on 2045-07-08 again showed endoscopic remission. On 2048-08-11, colonoscopy demonstrated deep remission; biopsies showed only rare cryptitis in the right colon and rare Paneth cell metaplasia in the left colon, without dysplasia or granulomas.

Inflammatory markers remained low for most of the record. CRP ranged from 0.4 to 2.3 mg/L through 2051, with ESR values generally between 1 and 13 mm/h. On 2051-12-02, CRP increased to 3.3 mg/L and ESR to 15 mm/h, suggesting a modest rise in inflammatory activity. Mild or borderline anemia recurred, with hemoglobin values between 11.5 and 13.2 g/dL. Vitamin D was deficient at 17 ng/mL on 2045-03-25 but later improved to 33 ng/mL.

## Latest flare and treatment access

By 2052-06-17, John Smith reported worsening symptoms after losing access to branded Lialda. Generic mesalamine was documented as ineffective, and he had been off effective mesalamine therapy for approximately two months because of insurance coverage. The clinical plan was to urgently resubmit authorization for branded Lialda, begin prednisone as a temporary bridge, obtain blood and stool studies, and consider escalation to guselkumab (Tremfya).

Testing ordered for the flare included CRP, fecal calprotectin, bacterial, viral, and parasite stool PCR panels, C. difficile testing, and hepatitis B screening. Tremfya authorization was approved on 2052-06-17, and branded Lialda was approved after urgent resubmission on 2052-06-18.

## Other relevant history

The record includes uveitis as an extraintestinal manifestation, lichen planus, and dermatologic diagnoses including lentigines, melanocytic nevi, seborrheic dermatitis, and photodermatitis. Immunizations documented include hepatitis A, hepatitis B, Tdap, influenza, COVID-19 vaccines, and Shingrix.
